coredata.dc:title: Serine and histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dot with unique double fluorescence emission as a fluorescent probe for highly sensitive detection of carbendazim
coredata.dc:creator: Ruiyi, Li
Yanhong, Jiang
Qinsheng, Wang
Yongqiang, Yang
Nana, Li
Xiulan, Sun
Zaijun, Li
coredata.dc:description: The application of graphene quantum dots has been focused on fluorescence detection, but poor functionality and low luminous efficiency limit its many applications. The study reports synthesis of serine and histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dot (Ser-GQD-His) via one-step simple pyrolysis. The resulting Ser-GQD-His offers an unique double fluorescence emission. Ultraviolet-light excitation at 360 nm produces one strong blue fluorescence with the quantum yield of 28.6 %. While visible-light excitation at 485 nm produces one stronger yellow fluorescence with the quantum yield of 51.2 %. The Ser-GQD-His was used as an optical probe for fluorescence detection of carbendazim coupled with DNA recycling amplification. By target-induced DNA recycling, one carbendazim molecule can lead to the formation of many G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzymes. The formed DNAzymes catalyze oxidation of phenylenediamine into 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) and achieves to a significant signal amplification. Meanwhile, the interaction of DAP with Ser-GQD-His leads to a fluorescence quenching process of Ser-GQD-His and fluorescence enhancement of DAP. This further enhances the detection signal. The ratiometric fluorescence signal increases linearly with increasing carbendazim in the range from 1.0 × 10−16 to 1.0 × 10−9 M with the limit of detection of 6.1 × 10−17 M. The proposed method exhibits a higher sensitivity and selectivity compared with reported methods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Functional graphene quantum dot
Yellow fluorescence
Organophosphorus pesticide
Fresh food

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (A), HRTEM (B), AFM images (C) and FTIR spectrum (D) of Ser-GQD-His.

The fluorescence emission spectra of His-GQD, Ser-GQD and Ser-GQD-His at ultraviolet-light excitation of 360 nm. Insert: The -hotographs of Ser-GQD-His aqueous solution in daylight (a) and ultraviolet-light excitation of 360 nm (b).

A: Fluorescence emission spectra of Ser-GQD-His at the excitation wavelengths of 340, 350, 360, 370, 380, 390, 400,410, 420, 430, 440, 450, 460, 470, 480, 485, 490 and 500 nm (from left to right). B: Relationship curves of excitation wavelength with corresponding peak fluorescence intensity (a) and maximum emission wavelength (b).
Fluorescence detection of carbendazim based on Ser-GQD-His and DNA cycle amplification.
The ΔF values for different reaction systems caused by 1 × 10−12 M carbendazim.
Relationship curves of ΔF value with H2O2 concentration (A) and hemin concentration (B).

A: Fluorescence spectra under visible excitation of 420 nm in the presence of 0.0, 1 × 10−16, 5 × 10−16, 1 × 10−15, 5 × 10−15, 1 × 10−14, 5 × 10−14, 1 × 10−13, 5 × 10−13, 1 × 10−12, 5 × 10−12, 1 × 10−11, 5 × 10−11, 1 × 10−10, 5 × 10−10, 1 × 10−9, 1 × 10−8, 1 × 10−7 and 1 × 10−6 M carbendazim (from bottom to top). B: Fluorescence spectra under ultraviolet excitation of 360 nm in the presence of 0.0, 1 × 10−16, 5 × 10−16, 1 × 10−15, 5 × 10−15, 1 × 10−14, 5 × 10−14, 1 × 10−13, 5 × 10−13, 1 × 10−12, 5 × 10−12, 1 × 10−11, 5 × 10−11, 1 × 10−10, 5 × 10−10, 1 × 10−9, 1 × 10−8, 1 × 10−7 and 1 × 10−6 M carbendazim (from bottom to top).C: Relationship curves of ΔFDAP and ΔFGQD with LOG[Ccarbendazim, M]. D: Relationship curve of ΔFDAP+GQD with LOG[Ccarbendazim, M].
The ΔFDAP+GQD values caused by addition of 1 × 10−12 M carbendazim, chlorpyrifos, carbofuran and imidacloprid.
Comparison of the proposed methods for detection of carbendazim with other reported methods.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The results for detection of carbendazim in tomato samples (N = 5).

head.ce:title: Serine and histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dot with unique double fluorescence emission as a fluorescent probe for highly sensitive detection of carbendazim

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Material Engineering
School of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Graphene Products Quality Supervision and Inspection Center (Jiangsu), Jiangsu Province Special Equipment Safety Supervision Inspection Institute·Branch of Wuxi, Wuxi, 214174, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Graphene Products Quality Supervision and Inspection Center (Jiangsu)
Jiangsu Province Special Equipment Safety Supervision Inspection Institute·Branch of Wuxi

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bNational Graphene Products Quality Supervision and Inspection Center (Jiangsu), Jiangsu Province Special Equipment Safety Supervision Inspection Institute·Branch of Wuxi, Wuxi 214174

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The application of graphene quantum dots has been focused on fluorescence detection, but poor functionality and low luminous efficiency limit its many applications. The study reports synthesis of serine and histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dot (Ser-GQD-His) via one-step simple pyrolysis. The resulting Ser-GQD-His offers an unique double fluorescence emission. Ultraviolet-light excitation at 360 nm produces one strong blue fluorescence with the quantum yield of 28.6 %. While visible-light excitation at 485 nm produces one stronger yellow fluorescence with the quantum yield of 51.2 %. The Ser-GQD-His was used as an optical probe for fluorescence detection of carbendazim coupled with DNA recycling amplification. By target-induced DNA recycling, one carbendazim molecule can lead to the formation of many G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzymes. The formed DNAzymes catalyze oxidation of phenylenediamine into 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) and achieves to a significant signal amplification. Meanwhile, the interaction of DAP with Ser-GQD-His leads to a fluorescence quenching process of Ser-GQD-His and fluorescence enhancement of DAP. This further enhances the detection signal. The ratiometric fluorescence signal increases linearly with increasing carbendazim in the range from 1.0 × 10−16 to 1.0 × 10−9 M with the limit of detection of 6.1 × 10−17 M. The proposed method exhibits a higher sensitivity and selectivity compared with reported methods.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbendazim has been widely used to control plant pathogens in fruit and vegetable crops [1]. Excellent chemical stability allows carbendazim to remain effective for a long time [2]. However, good stability leads to the accumulation of carbendazim in environment and presence as the residues of carbendazim in foods. The studies have demonstrated that carbendazim may cause serious health hazards even at a low level [3]. High level of carbendazim in food promotes the endocrine system disruption and even cancer [4]. Development of a reliable analytical method to monitor the carbendazim in environment and food is important to ensure food safety.

The techniques for detection of trace carbendazim are mainly high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [5], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LCMS) [6], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [7], colorimetric sensing [8], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [9], electrochemical sensing [10] and fluorescence method [11]. Among these methods, HPLC, LCMS and CE are classical analytical techniques for detection of fungicides in the laboratory. These techniques can provide reliable results, but require complex sample preparation, expensive apparatus and skilled operators [12]. Colorimetric sensing is simple, rapid and inexpensive, but it gives a relatively low sensitivity [13]. SERS has high sensitivity, but the instability of detection signal greatly limits its applications in the field of sample analysis. Electrochemical sensing has desired sensitivity, but many biomacromolecules, such as protein, nucleic acid and polysaccharide, and electro-actively small molecules, such as amino acid, ascorbic acid, citric acid and glucose, often interfere with the detection of carbendazim [14]. The fluorescence method has attracted increasing attention due to its high reaction speed, low cost and high sensitivity [15].

Graphene quantum dot (GQD) is an outstanding luminescent material with the advantages of photostability, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, low cost and easy synthesis. GQD also has special photoelectric property due to its quantum confinement and edge effect [16]. To date, GQD has shown a great potential for the applications in fluorescence detection and bioimaging. To meet the requirements of different applications, three strategies were used for the design and synthesis of GQDs [17]. The first strategy is to enhance the quantum yield of GQDs. The research has shown that doping element is an effective way to adjust the optical properties of GQDs. Su et al. synthesized a N-GQD using ethylenediamine, graphene oxide and hydrogen peroxide. The quantum yield of N-GQDs was 0.46, which is much higher that of common GQDs [18]. Moreover, the introduction of electron-donating group on the edge of graphene sheets can also improve the quantum yield of GQDs. Our group reported synthesis of pentaethylenehexamine (PEHA) and d-penicillamine-functionalized GQDs and their applications in the detection of Hg2+ and bioimaging [19]. The quantum yield of functionalized GQDs reached to 90.91 %, which is much better than that of other reported GQDs. This confirms that the introduction of PEHA and d-penicillamine significantly enhances the fluorescence emission. The second strategy is to functionalize GQDs by introducing special functional molecule on the edge of graphene sheets [20]. Our group reported synthesis and application of histidine-functionalized GQDs (His-GQDs) and folic acid-functionalized GQDs (FA-GQDs). His-GQDs are easy to coordinate with a metal ion to form a stable complex and were used in the fabrication of metal nanomaterials [21,22]. FA-GQDs can specifically recognize cancer cells and was successfully used for electrochemical detection of cancer cells [23] and the targeted delivery of cancer drugs [24]. The third strategy is to tailor the fluorescence emission of GQDs [25,26]. Gu et al. synthesized a N-GQD with tailoring color emissions by using different reaction media [27]. However, poor functionality and low luminous efficiency still limit many applications of GQDs.

In this study, we designed and synthesized serine and histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dot (Ser-GQD-His). The resulting Ser-GQD-His shows an unique double fluorescence emission. Ultravoilet-light excitation of 360 nm produces one strong blue fluorescence. While visible-light excitation of 485 nm produces one stronger yellow fluorescence. The Ser-GQD-His probe were used for fluorescence detection of carbendazim. The analytical method provides the advantages of sensitivity and specificity.

Serine, histidine, hemin, citric acid and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. LLC (Shanghai, China). The pesticides used were purchased from Beijing YiHua TongBiao technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). All oligonucleotides used, including the aptamer (AP) (5′-GGGCA CACAA CAACC GATGG TCCAG CCACC CGAAT GACCA GCCCA CCCGC CACCC CGCG-3′), assistant strand (AS) (5′-TCACA GCGGG TGGGC TGGTC-3′), hairpin DNA 1 (H1) (5′-GCCCA CCCGC TGTGA ATCGA CTGAC GGCAA GG CGTAG TGGGT GGGTGG GTGGG-3′) and hairpin DNA 2 (H2) (5′-CACCC TTGCC GTCAG TCGAT TCACA GCGG TGGCAA GGGTG GGTGG GCGGG-3′), were synthesized and purified by Sangon Biotech. Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The DNA stock solution was prepared in tris-EDTA (TE) buffer (10 mM Tris-HCI, 12.5 mM MgCl2 and 1 mM EDTA, at pH 8.0), and stored at −20 °C. The H1 and H2 were pretreated by heating at 95 °C for 5 min and cooling to 37 °C. Phosphate-buffered solution (PBS) (Na2HPO4-NaH2PO4-NaCl, 0.1 M, pH 7.4), MES buffer (0.1 M MES, 0.5 M NaCl, pH 6) and ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) were used in all experiments.

Citric acid (0.1 mol) and serine (0.02 mol) were first mixed with histidine (0.1 mol) in a 250-mL glass beaker L, dissolved in water and dried to remove all free water. The beaker was placed in a high-temperature oven, set at 190 °C, for 3 h and then cooled to ambient temperature. The obtained crude Ser-GQD-His preparation was dissolved in water to make a Ser-GQD-His solution (50 mg mL−1). To obtain a solid Ser-GQD-His sample, the collected solution was dialyzed to remove unreacted citric acid, serine and histidine and ultimately dried by freeze-drying.

The fresh tomato sample used was collected from a market in Wuxi city (China) and homogenized in a disintegrator. A 2-g sample was transferred into a 10-mL centrifuge tube, extracted three times ultrasonically with a solvent mixture of acetonitrile/H2O/HAc (v/v, 1:1:1) and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min, in sequence. The collected supernatant was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 5 min and the degreased phase was removed. The obtained sample solution was concentrated to 10 mL for fluorescence detection of carbendazim.

AP (25 μL, 500 nM), AS (25 μL, 500 nM) and 230 μL of PBS were transferred into a 1.5-mL centrifuge tube. The mixed solution was incubated at 37 °C for 30 min and then 80 μL of carbendazim standard solution or sample solution was added. After incubating at 37 °C for 30 min, the pretreated H1 (25 μL, 1 μM) and pretreated H2 (25 μL, 1 μM) were added and incubated at 37 °C for 2 h. Subsequently, the hemin solution (50 μL, 0.4 μM) was added and the solution mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. Then, the OPD (100 μL, 80 mM), Ser-GQD-His (160 μL, 1 mg mL−1) and H2O2 solution (80 μL, 40 mM) were added and the solution mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 30 min in sequence. The resulting solution was subjected to fluorescence measurement on a fluorophotometer [28].

Ser-GQD-His was made by one-step pyrolysis of the mixture of serine, histidine and citric acid (Fig. 1 ). Citric acid was used for making graphene sheets. Citric acid molecule can be changed into graphene sheet at a relatively high temperature, because of its special molecular structure and chemical properties [26]. Serine and histidine were selected as the functional reagents to improve the optical properties of GQD and catalytic activity of hemin. Because serine molecule contains one aliphatic hydroxyl group, the introduction of serine on the edge of graphene sheets enhances the charge density of graphene sheets due to the strong electron-donating ability of hydroxyl. This increases the luminous efficacy of GQD. The previous studies have demonstrated that histidine can enhance the catalytic activity of hemin [29]. Accordingly, the introduced of histidine improves the catalytic activity of G-quadruplex/hemin for oxidization of OPD with H2O2. This leads to the formation of more fluorescent 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) molecules, and an enhanced fluorescence signal. As serine and histidine have different structures and physicochemical properties, their synergy may improve the optical performance of GQDs to meet the requirements of fluorescence detection. The as-designed GQDs were prepared by heating the mixture of serine, histidine and citric acid at 190 °C for 3 h.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy were used to characterize the structure of Ser-GQD-His (Fig. 2 ). The as-synthesized Ser-GQD-His is composed of tiny graphene sheets with the size of 3.4 nm. The crystal spacing of 0.21 nm on the high-resolution TEM image (HRTEM) is assigned to the (100) crystal plane of graphene. The thickness of Ser-GQD-His is 1.1 nm, demonstrating that Ser-GQD-His contains 3–4 layers of graphene sheets. The FTIR spectrum includes many IR absorption peaks and absorption bands. The absorption peak at 3409 cm−1 is due to the symmetric vibration of NH bond in NH2. The peak at 1697 cm-1 is ascribed to the IR absorption caused by the stretching vibration of CO bond in amide. The peak at 1604 cm-1 is owe to the bending vibration of NH bond plane in the primary amine as well as the asymmetric stretching vibration of CO bond. In addition, there are many IR absorption peaks between 1700 and 500 cm-1, which correspond to the IR absorptions induced by the symmetric vibration of imidazole framework structure.

To test the stability of Ser-GQD-His, the 1.0 mg mL−1 Ser-GQD-His aqueous solution was stored at 25 °C and 1 atom for 100-day and then measured its fluorescence emission. The result shows the fluorescence emission of Ser-GQD-His almost remain unchanged after a 100-day storage. Therefore, the Ser-GQD-His aqueous solution can store at room temperature.

To evalucate the fluorescence property of Ser-GQD-His, the fluorescence characteristics of His-GQD, Ser-GQD and Ser-GQD-His were independently studied. Fig. 3 shows different materials display different fluorescence behaviours. The His-GQD offers one blue fluorescence with the maximum emission wavelength of 430 nm under the ultraviolet-light excitation of 360 nm. The intensity is weaker than that of Ser-GQD and Ser-GQD-His. Similar to His-GQD, Ser-GQD exhibits one blue fluorescence with the maximum emission of 470 nm. However, its fluorescence intensity is higher than that of His-GQD. The increased fluorescence intensity is mainly attributed to the electron-donating ability of hydroxyl group. Different from His-GQD and Ser-GQD, Ser-GQD-His shows an unique double fluorescence emission. There are one blue fluorescence emission peak at 450 nm and one yellow fluorescence emission at 550 nm under the ultraviolet-light excitation of 360 nm.

The fluorescence spectra of Ser-GQD-His at different excitation wavelengths and the relationship curves of the excitation wavelength with the peak fluorescence intensity and the maximum emission wavelength are shown in Fig. 4 . The fluorescence emission of Ser-GQD-His strongly depends on the corresponding excitation wavelength. The excitation wavelength affects not only the emission wavelength, but also the luminescence intensity. The maximum emission wavelength rapidly increases from 440 to 530 nm with the increase of the excitation wavelength when the excitation wavelength is lower than 420 nm. However, the maximum emission wavelength tends to be stable when the excitation wavelength is higher than 420 nm. Different from GQD (Fig. s1), the relationship curve of the excitation wavelength with the maximum fluorescence intensity shows two peaks at the excitation wavelengths of 360 and 485 nm. The fluorescence intensity increases with the increase of the excitation wavelength when the excitation wavelength is lower than 360 nm. The intensity reaches its maximum value at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm and then decreases with increasing excitation wavelength. When the excitation wavelength is lower than 485 nm, the intensity drops to its minimum at the excitation wavelength of 400 nm and then increases with increasing excitation wavelength. The intensity reaches to its maximum value at the excitation wavelength of 485 nm and then decreases with the increase of the excitation wavelength. As a result, the UV excitation at 360 nm produces a strong blue fluorescence and the visible-light excitation at a wavelength of 485 nm produces a strong yellow fluorescence. Noteworthy, the intensity of its yellow emission is higher than that of its blue emission.

The excitation spectra and emission spectra of Ser-GQD-His were measured at the UV-light excitation wavelength of 360 nm and visible-light excitation wavelength of 485 nm. At the UV-light excitation wavelegth of 360 nm, the excitation spectrum is in a wavelength range from 300 to 420 nm. The wavelength is clearly higher than that of organic small molecules. This indicates the formation of graphene sheets with a large π-electron conjugated system by the pyrolysis of serine, histidine and citric acid. The fluorescence emission spectrum is between 390 and 650 nm with a maximum emission peak at 450 nm, indicating a strong blue fluorescence (Fig. s2A). The blue fluorescence emission exhibits a higher intensity compared with those of the GQDs, His-GQDs, Ser-GQDs and Ser-GQD-His (Fig. s2B). The intensity is more than 5.2-fold that of His-GQDs, 1.8-fold that of GQDs and 1.3-fold that of Ser-GQDs. At the visible-light excitation of 485 nm, the fluorescence emission spectrum is between 500 and 640 nm with a maximum emission peak at 550 nm, indicating strong yellow fluorescence (Fig. s2C). The fluorescence intensity is higher than 166-fold that of GQDs, 163-fold that of His-GQDs and 31-fold that of Ser-GQDs (Fig. s2D). The yellow fluorescence emission provides a greater advantage over the blue fluorescence emission. Further, the fluorescence quantum yield values of Ser-GQD-His were meausred by using a reported method [30]. The quantum yield values are 28.6 % for the blue fluorescence at the UV-light excitation wavelength of 360 nm and 51.2 % for the yellow fluorescence at visible-light excitation wavelength of 485 nm.

To understand the role of serine in improving the optical properties of the probe, a series of Ser-GQD-His samples were prepared by varying the molar ratio of serine/citric acid (Ser/CA). Their blue emission and yellow emission behaviors were measured. Fig. s3 indicates the fluorescence spectrum displays an obvious double fluorescence emission when the Ser/CA ratio is less than 0.2 at the UV excitation of 360 nm. However, the characteristic double fluorescence emission disappeared when the Ser/CA ratio is more than 0.2. The spectrum displayed in Fig. s3B shows that the intensity of the blue fluorescence increases with the increase of the Ser/CA ratio. The increase slows down and tends to stabilize when the Ser/CA ratio is more than 0.8. At the visible-light excitation wavelength of 485 nm, all fluorescence emission spectra have a similar shape. However, the intensity will rapidly increase with the increase of the Ser/CA ratio, because the emission intensity depends on the molar ratio of Ser/CA. The intensity reaches its maximum at the Ser/CA ratio of 0.2 and then decreases with the increase of the Ser/CA ratio when the ratio is more than 0.2. Consequently, the unique optical property of Ser-GQD-His is mainly due to the introduction of serine and histidine.

To examine the photostability, the quartz cuvette filled with Ser-GQD-His aqeuous solution was placed under Xenon lamp irradiation (300 W) for 120 min and its fluorescence was measured on the fluorescence spectrophotometer every 10 min. The result indicates the fluorescence remains stable within the irradiating time of 120 min. This verifies that the Ser-GQD-His has high photostability.

The Ser-GQD-His was designed as an optical probe for fluorescence detection of carbendazim coupled with DNA cycling amplification (Fig. 5 ). First, carbendazim aptamer (AP) specificaly binds with assistant strand (AS) to form duplex DNA (AP-AS). Then, carbendazim binds with the formed AP-AS to release AS. The released AS triggers the DNA recycling process. In the recycling, the AS hybridizes with hairpin DNA 1 (H1) to form duplex DNA (AS-H1). Hairpin DNA 2 (H2) replaces the AS in formed AS-H1 and leads to the formation of G-quadruplex-duplex DNA (H1-H2). As H2 contains more bases that can pair with H1, the H1-H2 has a better stability compared with AS-H1. The difference in stability between H1-H2 and AS-H1 eventually leads to the formation of H1-H2 and release of AS. Simultaneously, one AS is released from the AS-H1 and is directly used for the next DNA cycle. The use of AS-induced recycling allows a carbendazim molecule to produce many H1-H2 molecules. The formed H1-H2 molecules combine with hemin molecules to produce the hemin DNAzyme (G4/hemin). With the G4/hemin as catalyts, the OPD is oxidized to form the fluorescent oxidation product DAP, causing the fluorescence to change from the “OFF-state” to “ON-state” (OFF-ON state) (shown in Fig. s4). This approach achieves a suitable signal amplification. In the presence of the Ser-GQD-His probe, DAP interacts with this probe, thereby quenching the fluorescence of Ser-GQD-His, and enhancing its fluorescence intensity, ultimately causing the fluorescence to change from the “ON-state” to the “OFF-state” (ON-OFF state) (shown in Fig. s5). This “ON-OFF state” change further enhances the detection signal. In addition, the Ser-GQD-His probe improves the catalytic activity of the G4/hemin due to the introduction of histidine. This accelerates the oxidation of OPD and results in the increased fluorescence intensity of DAP.

In order to understand the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the SER-GQD-His probe, we measured the fluorescence emission spectrum of the Ser-GQD-His probe and the absorption spectrum of DAP with or without DAP. The spectra displayed in Fig. s6A reveal that there is a large overlap between the emission spectrum of the Ser-GQD-His probe and the absorption spectrum of DAP in the wavelength range of 390−470 nm, which indicates an energy transfer process from Ser-GQD-His into DAP. The energy transfer results in an obvious quenching of the blue fluorescence signal of the Ser-GQD-His probe and fluorescence enhancement of DAP. The fluorescence attenuation effect of the Ser-GQD-His probe in the presence and absence of DAP was further studied. The results in Fig. s6B indicate that in the absence of DAP the average fluorescence lifetime of the Ser-GQD-His probe is about 7.61 ns. The lifetime decreases to 5.52 ns in the presence of DAP, indicating a reduced fluorescence lifetime. The results show that the fluorescence quenching of the Ser-GQD-His probe is a dynamic quenching process. Since DAP is an electron acceptor, during the energy transfer between DAP and the Ser-GQD-His probe, the photo-induced electrons are transferred from the Ser-GQD-His probe to DAP. Based on the above results, we suggest that the fluorescence quenching of the Ser-GQD-His probe by DAP is due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between these two molecules [31].

Six kinds of reaction systems were designed and used to study on the effects of several important components of the sensing platform shown in Fig. 5 on the fluorescence detection of carbendazim, including the reaction system lacking only the AS (system I), the reaction system lacking only H1 (system II), the reaction system lacking only H2 (system III), the reaction system lacking only hemin (system IV), the reaction system lacking only Ser-GQD-His (system V) and the complete reaction system (system VI). First, all reaction systems were operated according to the same procedure described in section 2.3 for the detection of 1 × 10−12 M carbendazim. Then, their change (ΔF) values in the peak fluorescence intensity of DAP and Ser-GQD-His were measured and calculated, and the results are shown in Fig. 6 . In the absense of AS, H1 or H2, the target-induced DNA recycling cannot be triggered. Since no G4/hemin DNAzyme is produced, the oxidation of OPD into DAP is very slow due to the poor catalytic activity of hemin. As a result, the DAP exhibits an extremely weak fluorescence intensity due to its low concentration. Thus, system I, system II and system III only produce a very small ΔF value. In the absence of hemin, the target-induced DNA recycling can be triggered. Although a lot of H1-H2 hybrids are produced in the detection process, their poor catalytic activity leads to the formation of very few DAP moclecules, as a result the system IV ;produces a small ΔF value. In the absence of the Ser-GQD-His probe, the target DNA recycling process can be well triggerred. A carbendazim molecule can produce many G4/hemin composites by the DNA recycling process, which catalyze the oxidation of OPD into DAP. This leads to the enhancement of the fluorescence emission of DAP. Thus, the system V achieves a much higher ΔF value compared with that achieved by system I, system II, system III and system IV. However, the ΔF value is noticeably smaller than that of system VI. This is due to the improvement of the catalytic activity by the Ser-GQD-His probe, which causes the fluorescence quenching of Ser-GQD-His and enhancement of DAP through the interaction between DAP and Ser-GQD-His. These factors grealty enhance the sensitivity for detection of carbendazim.

To optimize the detection conditions, the effects of the concentration of H2O2 and hemin as well as the incubation time on the fluorescence reponse (ΔF) of the proposed sensing platform to 1 × 10−12 M carbendazim were independently investigated. The results in Fig. 7 A show that the ΔF value will rapidly increase with increasing H2O2 concentration if the concentration is lower than 40 mM. Since H2O2 is a strong oxidant, it can oxidize OPD into DAP under the catalysis of G4/hemin. A higher concentration of H2O2 results in the formation of more DAP molecules, which leads to an increased ΔF value. However, when the H2O2 concentration is above 40 mM, the ΔF value tends to be stable with increasing H2O2 concentration. The concentration of 40 mM is sufficient to meet the H2O2 demand of the reaction system, thus increasing the H2O2 concentration does not improve the fluorescence response. Consequently, the ΔF value does not change significantly with increasing H2O2 concentration. The relationship curve of the hemin concentration, shown Fig. 7B, reveals that when the hemin concentration is less than 0.4 mM, the ΔF will rapidly increase with increasing hemin concentration. This is because hemin is an important component of the G4/hemin DNAzyme and more hemin molecules result in more DNAzyme composites, which accelerates the oxidation of OPD and produces more DAP molecules, indicating a stronger fluorescence of DAP. However, when the hemin concentration is more than 0.4 mM the ΔF value remains stable. In the presence of 0.4 mM hemin, all H1-H2 duplex DNA hybrids in the system can combine with hemin to form the G4/hemin DNAzyme composites. When the amount of G4/hemin reaches its maximum, the number of formed DAP molecules remains almost constant. At this state, the ΔF value does not continue to increase with the increase of hemin. Thus, the H2O2 concentration of 40 mM and hemin concentration of 0.4 mM are selected for the fluorescence detection of carbendazim to obtain a high sensitivity.

Fig. s7 presents the relationship curve of ΔF value with the incubation time after added OPD, Ser-GQD-His and H2O2. With the increase of incubation time, the ΔF value will increase when the incubation time is less than 20 min. However, the ΔF value tends to stable and then remains unchanged when the inclubation time is more then 20 min. This verifies that the reaction reaches to the equilibrium state. To obtain high sensitivity and save the analysis time, the incubation time of 30 min was employed for detection of carbendazim.

Fluorescence characteristics of the proposed sensing system were studied by varying the carbendazim concentration. The fluorescence spectra obtained after visible-lightexcitation at a wavelength of 420 nm and a UV-light excitation at a wavelength of 360 nm in the presence of different concentrations of carbendazim are shown in Fig. 8 . With increasing the carbendazim concentration, the peak fluorescence intensity produced by the excitation at a wavelength of 420 nm will rapidly increase (Fig. 8A). This is due to the higher concentration of carbendazim, which ultimately leads to the formation of more DAP molecules. This brings an increased fluorescence intensity of DAP. However, the intensity of the fluorescence emission caused by the UV-light excitation at a wavelength of 360 nm decreases with the increase of the carbendazim concentration. This is due to fluorescence quenching produced by the interaction between DAP and the Ser-GQD-His probe. A higher concentration of carbendazim results in the production of more DAP molecules, which leads to a greater degree of fluorescence quenching of the Ser-GQD-His probe. The above results demonstrate that the change in the fluorescence signals of DAP and the Ser-GQD-His probe depend only on the concentration of carbendazim. Therefore, the signal changes of both DAP and the Ser-GQD-His probe can be directly used for the fluorescence detection of carbendazim. Additionally, the relationship curves of the change in the fluorescence intensity caused by DAP (ΔFDAP) and the Ser-GQD-His probe (ΔFGQD) with the logarithm of the carbendazim concentration (LOG[Ccarbendazim,M]) are shown in Fig. 8C and D. When the carbendazim concentration is in the range from 1.0 × 10−16 to 1.0 × 10−9 M, there is a good linear relationship between the LOG[Ccarbendazim, M] and ΔFDAP, ΔFGQD or the sum of ΔFDAP and ΔFGQD (ΔFDAP+GQD). Remarkably, the relationship curve of ΔFDAP+GQD with LOG[Ccarbendazim, M] gives a steeper slope, indicating an increased sensitivity for the detection of carbendazim. For this reason, the ΔFDAP+GQD is used as the detection signal of the proposed sensing platform for carbendazim. The corresponding linear correlation equation is as follows: ΔFDAP+GQD = 68.138xLOG[Ccarbendazim, M]+1144.2 with a regression coefficient (R2) of 0.992. The detection limit was found to be 6.1 × 10−17 M (S/N = 3), confirming that the proposed method has a much higher sensitivity compared with all reported methods for the detection of carbendazim, as shown in Table 1 [32–40]. In addition, the evaluation of the repeatability of the proposed sensing platform by testing twenty individual detections of 1.0 × 10−12 M carbendazim revealed a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.8 %, indicating an acceptable repeatability.

Carbendazim, chlorpyrifos, carbofuran and imidacloprid are commonly used fungicides in tomato crops. Their presence may interfere with the detection of carbendazim due to their similar chemical properties. To determine the specificity of the proposed method, the effect of carbendazim, chlorpyrifos, carbofuran and imidacloprid on the fluorescence response were evaluated. The results presented in Fig. 9 indicate that only carbendazim produces a sensitive fluorescence response demonstrating that the proposed method has good specificity. This is mainly attributed to design of the base sequences of the AS and AP. Only carbendazim can firmly hybridize with the AP by complementary base pairing. Such characteristic determines the fluorescence response of the sensing system strictly depends on the concentration of carbendazim. On the other hand, carbendazim binds with AP-AS to release free AS. The released AS triggers the DNA recycling process. Through the DNA recycling process, a carbendazim molecule results in the production of many G4/hemin DNAzyme composites. These DNAzymes catalyze the oxidation of OPD into the fluorescent DAP molecule and achieves significant fluorescence signal amplification, which in the presence of carbendazim produces a large change in the fluorescence signal. However, other pesticides do not specificially bind with AP due to lack of base complementarity. In the presence of chlorpyrifos, carbofuran and imidacloprid, the target-induced DNA recycling cannot occur during the incubation. Thus, their addition only results in some non-specific fluorescence response. Compared with the fluorescence signal generated by carbendazim, the above non-specific fluorescence signal is extremely low. Therefore, the as-proposed sensing platform can be directly used for the detection of carbendazim in tomato.

The analytical method was used for the fluorescence detection of carbendazim in tomato samples. The recovery experiment was carried out by adding a known concentration of the standard carbendazim into tomato juice prepared from a tomato sample. The spiked concentration of carbendazim used was based on information from experimental data [41]. Table 2 indicates that the recovery of carbendazim in tomato samples is between 95 and 105 %. The results obtained with the proposed method are well consistent with those obtained by the HPLC-MS method, indicating outstanding accuracy and reliability.

This study has demonstrated the synthesis of serine- and histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots with unique double fluorescence emission. UV-light excitation at a wavelength of 360 nm produces a strong blue fluorescence emission and visible-light excitation at a wavelength of 485 nm produces a stronger yellow fluorescence emission. The blue and yellow emissions were used for fluorescence detection of carbendazim coupled with target-induced DNA recycling amplification and cell imaging, respectively. The analytical method exhibits ultra-high sensitivity and selectivity for fluorescence detection of carbendazim. The functionalized graphene quantum dots can also be applied in various fluorescence detection and bioimaging.

Li Ruiyi: Investigation. Jiang Yanhong: Visualization, Investigation. Wang Qinsheng: Visualization. Yang Yongqiang: Investigation. Li Nana: Investigation. Sun Xiulan: Investigation. Li Zaijun: Conceptualization, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support from National Key Research and Development Program of China (2018YFC1603001), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (JUSRP12050) and Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province Special Equipment Safety Supervision Inspection Institute (KJ(Y)2020037).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.130099.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Li Ruiyi is currently an associate professor in School of School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Jiangnan University, China. She received his master degree from The Birmingham University, The United Kingdom, 2014. Her research interest is synthesis of graphene-based nanomaterials and their application in electrochemical sensors.

Jiang Yanhong is currently a master candidate in School of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Jiangnan University, China. Her research interest is synthesis of graphene quantum dots and their application in optical sensors.

Wang Qinsheng is National Graphene Product Quality Supervision and Inspection Center, Jiangsu Province Special Equipment Safety Supervision and Inspection Institute Branch, China. He received his master degree from Nanjing University Of Science and Technology, China, in 2006. His current research interests are synthesis and structure characterization of graphene-based nanomaterials

Yang Yongqiang is National Graphene Product Quality Supervision and Inspection Center, Jiangsu Province Special Equipment Safety Supervision and Inspection Institute Branch, China. He received his doctor degree from Shanghai Jiao Tong University, China, in 2014. His current research interests are synthesis and structure characterization of graphene-based nanomaterials.

Li Nana is currently a doctor candidate in School of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Jiangnan University, China. Her research interest is synthesis of graphene nanohybrids and application in electrochemical sensors.

Sun Xiulan is a professor of School of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University, China. She received his Doctor degree from Jiangnan University, China, in 2006. She is author of more than 100 papers. She current research interests are food saftey.

Li Zaijun is a professor of School of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Jiangnan University, China. He received his doctor degree from Jiangnan University, China, in 2004. He is author of more than 200 papers and reviewer of more than 40 journals. His current research interests are synthesis of graphene-based nanomaterials and their applications in sensing, electrocatalysis and energy storage/conversion devices.
